text: The surgeon begins the dissection using hot shears at port R-I, a vessel sealer at port R-II, and a tip-up prograsp at port R-III. The hepatic flexure of the colon is visualized and mobilized as part of the initial dissection phase.

The surgeon begins dissection at the right ascending colon, following the white line of Toldt, and proceeds to medially mobilize the ascending colon. The hepatic flexure is mobilized first, with the underlying duodenum becoming visible as the dissection progresses towards the right lateral parietal wall.
The surgeon meticulously dissects around the duodenum using monopolar hot shears, taking care to avoid iatrogenic injury during the procedure.

The right branch of the middle colic vein is visualized, with the duodenum and head of the pancreas clearly visible below. The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the greater omentum from the mesocolon, revealing the branching pattern of the middle colic vein.

The surgeon separates the cecum from the white line of Toldt in the right iliac fossa, identifying the right-sided gonadal vessels and ureter. The dissection progresses cranially, mobilizing the terminal ileum. The cecum and appendix are lifted for better visualization.

The surgeon flips the bowel to locate the ileocolic pedicle, which is then ligated using a vessel sealer. Subsequently, the right colic pedicle is ligated, and the right branch of the middle colic pedicle is brought into view and similarly ligated with the vessel sealer.

The surgeon lifts the entire transverse colon and proceeds with dissection towards the left side, mobilizing the splenic flexure of the colon. This mobilization reduces tension on the anastomosis, completing the mobilization from the terminal ileum to the splenic flexure.
